title: What Is Wholesale Real Estate? A Complete Beginner's Guide
excerpt: Wholesale real estate is one of the fastest ways to break into property investing with little to no money down. This guide breaks down exactly how it works, what you can earn, and how to land your first deal.
author: AIWholesail Team
metaDescription: Learn what wholesale real estate is, how it works, what wholesalers earn, and step-by-step how to find and close your first deal in 2024.
metaKeywords: what is wholesale real estate, real estate wholesaling, how to wholesale real estate, wholesale deals, assignment of contract, real estate investing for beginners

sections.content: If you have ever heard someone say they flipped a contract instead of a house, they were probably talking about wholesale real estate. It is one of the most talked-about strategies in the investing world, and for good reason. You do not need a real estate license, a big bank account, or years of experience to get started. But before you dive in, you need to understand exactly what wholesale real estate is, how the money works, and what the process looks like from start to finish.
What Is Wholesale Real Estate?

Wholesale real estate is a short-term investment strategy where an investor — called a wholesaler — finds a deeply discounted property, gets it under contract, and then sells that contract to another buyer for a profit. The wholesaler never actually buys the home. Instead, they sell their right to purchase it. The difference between the price they locked in with the seller and the price they charge the end buyer is called the assignment fee, and that is where the wholesaler gets paid.

Here is a simple example. A wholesaler finds a distressed home worth $200,000 after repairs. They negotiate a purchase contract with the motivated seller for $120,000. They then find a house flipper willing to buy the contract for $135,000. The wholesaler collects a $15,000 assignment fee at closing without ever owning the property. The flipper gets a deal with enough room to renovate and profit, the seller gets a fast cash sale, and the wholesaler walks away with a check. Everyone wins.
How Does the Wholesale Process Work?

The wholesale process has a few key stages. Each one requires focus and hustle, but none of them requires a real estate license or a mortgage approval. Here is a step-by-step breakdown of how a typical wholesale deal comes together.
Step 1: Find a Motivated Seller

The foundation of every wholesale deal is a motivated seller — someone who needs to sell quickly and is willing to accept less than market value. These sellers are often facing foreclosure, divorce, job loss, inherited properties they do not want, or homes in poor condition that they cannot afford to fix. Finding these sellers is the hardest part of wholesaling. Common methods include direct mail campaigns, driving for dollars, cold calling, bandit signs, and online lead generation tools.
Step 2: Analyze the Deal

Once you have a potential lead, you need to run the numbers fast. The most important figure is the After Repair Value, or ARV. This is what the home will be worth once it is fully renovated. From there, wholesalers use a simple formula: ARV multiplied by 70 percent, minus estimated repair costs, equals the maximum allowable offer (MAO). If a home has an ARV of $200,000 and needs $30,000 in repairs, your MAO is $110,000. Offering anything above that gives your end buyer too little room to profit.

Use the free ARV Calculator at aiwholesail.com/tools/arv-calculator to instantly estimate a property's after repair value before making your offer. Getting this number wrong is the most common mistake new wholesalers make.
Step 3: Get the Property Under Contract

If the numbers work, you make an offer and get the seller to sign a purchase and sale agreement. This contract gives you the right to buy the property at the agreed price, and it also includes an assignability clause — which is what allows you to legally transfer the contract to another buyer. Without this clause, you cannot wholesale the deal. Make sure your contract language is reviewed by a real estate attorney familiar with wholesale transactions in your state.
Step 4: Find a Cash Buyer

Now you need to find an end buyer — usually a house flipper or landlord — who wants the deal. Most experienced wholesalers build a cash buyers list over time. You can find buyers at local real estate investment association (REIA) meetings, on Facebook groups, through direct outreach, or by networking at auctions. The faster your buyers list grows, the faster you can close deals. Some wholesalers close within 7 to 14 days of finding a motivated seller.
Step 5: Assign the Contract and Get Paid

Once you have a buyer, you sign an assignment of contract agreement. This document transfers your rights to purchase the property to the new buyer in exchange for your assignment fee. At closing, the title company or closing attorney disburses your fee — typically ranging from $5,000 to $20,000 or more depending on the deal. You collect your check and move on to the next deal.
How Much Do Wholesalers Actually Make?

Assignment fees vary widely based on the market, the deal, and how well the wholesaler negotiated. Entry-level deals in smaller markets might net $3,000 to $7,000. In competitive urban markets, fees of $15,000 to $30,000 are common. Some wholesalers doing high-volume operations close 5 to 10 deals per month, generating six-figure annual incomes. But it is important to be realistic — your first few deals will take longer and earn less while you build your skills and contacts.
Wholesaling is not a get-rich-quick scheme. It is a skill-based business that rewards consistency, hustle, and a deep understanding of your local market.
Who Is Wholesale Real Estate Best For?

Wholesaling is a great fit for people who are new to real estate investing and want to learn the business without risking a lot of capital. It is also popular among those who are good at sales, negotiation, and building relationships. You do not need cash to close deals because you are selling the contract, not the house. However, you do need time, energy, and persistence. Marketing costs money, and it can take weeks of outreach before your first lead converts.

items: New investors who want to learn real estate without buying property
People with strong sales and negotiation skills
Entrepreneurs who want a flexible, scalable income stream
Investors looking to build a cash buyers network for future deals
Anyone who wants to earn income before transitioning into flipping or rentals

sections.content: Common Mistakes to Avoid

items: Overestimating ARV and leaving the buyer with no profit margin
Underestimating repair costs when calculating your maximum allowable offer
Using contracts without an assignability clause
Building no buyers list before locking up deals
Skipping due diligence on title issues or liens
Operating without understanding your state's wholesaling laws

sections.content: Is Wholesale Real Estate Legal?

Yes, wholesale real estate is legal in all 50 states, but the rules around it have tightened in recent years. Some states require wholesalers to hold a real estate license if they are marketing a property they do not own, rather than just assigning the contract. Illinois, Oklahoma, and a handful of other states have introduced specific regulations around wholesaling. Always consult a real estate attorney in your state before starting, and stay current on local laws to avoid fines or legal trouble.

Before marketing any deal, make sure your purchase contract is in place and you have an equitable interest in the property. Marketing a home you have no contract on is where wholesalers most often run into legal problems.
Using Technology to Find Better Wholesale Deals

The days of manually driving neighborhoods and sending out thousands of mailers are not gone, but they have been supercharged by technology. Instead of spending hours pulling comps and researching ownership records, modern wholesalers use smart tools to do the heavy lifting so they can focus on what actually moves deals — conversations and relationships.
